summary: The post proposes an 'Inverse Web' - a peer-to-peer, encrypted, stateful alternative to the current client/server web architecture where corporations stand between users' communications. Inspired by NSA surveillance revelations and corporate control of communication, the author outlines four criteria for this inverted model and introduces Firestr, his open-source project implementing these principles.
key_points: The modern web operates as a star network with corporations at the center, despite TCP/IP being inherently decentralized
The Dot Com bubble's investment in HTTP/browser technology locked us into client/server architectures
The Inverse Web should be peer-to-peer, encrypted by default, stateful, and connect known people rather than anonymous servers
Firestr is a working implementation of these principles with encrypted P2P communication
Society can choose to fund decentralized alternatives without relying on VC and Silicon Valley
money_quotes: Great power plus great incompetency leads to great abuse.
Instead of browsers downloading software from anonymous servers, software is uploaded to people you know.
They stand in the middle of you and me. This position has allowed greater power and control of our communications and abuse by these companies.
We don't have to rely on the VCs and Valley to fund it. We can start another experiment in political economy.
key_insight: The web's client/server architecture has been co-opted into centralized star networks that enable surveillance and corporate control, and we need to invert this model toward peer-to-peer, encrypted communication systems built on the internet's inherently decentralized foundation.
